title: Allergic Rhinitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Primary Care, ENT
objectives: Classify allergic rhinitis by temporal pattern (intermittent vs persistent) and severity
Understand IgE-mediated pathophysiology and the allergic march
Apply stepwise pharmacotherapy per ARIA 2024-2025 guidelines
Compare intranasal corticosteroids vs oral antihistamines

guidelineReferences.name: ARIA-EAACI Guidelines 2024-2025
keyPoints: INCS are first-line for moderate-severe AR
INCS + INAH combination (MPAzeFlu) for inadequate response
Second-generation oral antihistamines for mild symptoms

vignette.text:
A 24-year-old woman presents with 5 years of nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhea, sneezing fits (10-15 sneezes in a row), and itchy eyes. Symptoms occur year-round but worsen in spring. She has tried OTC loratadine with partial relief. Symptoms significantly impact her work productivity and sleep.

Past Medical History: Childhood eczema (resolved), intermittent asthma (uses albuterol 2x/month)

Family History: Father with hay fever, brother with asthma

Medications: Loratadine 10mg PRN, albuterol PRN

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.general: Well-appearing woman, frequent nose rubbing (allergic salute)

HEENT.nose: Pale, boggy, bluish turbinates bilaterally; clear watery discharge; transverse nasal crease
HEENT.eyes: Bilateral conjunctival injection, chemosis, dark infraorbital circles (allergic shiners)

questions.stem: This patient has symptoms year-round with spring worsening.
questions.question: How is allergic rhinitis classified?

options.text: ARIA Classification by DURATION: INTERMITTENT: <4 days/week OR <4 consecutive weeks; PERSISTENT: ≥4 days/week AND ≥4 consecutive weeks; By SEVERITY: MILD: Normal sleep, normal daily activities, normal work/school, symptoms not troublesome; MODERATE-SEVERE: Any of - abnormal sleep, impairment of activities, impaired work/school, troublesome symptoms; This patient: PERSISTENT (year-round) + MODERATE-SEVERE (sleep/work impact)
Classification doesn't affect treatment
Severity based only on symptom count
Only seasonal vs perennial classification exists

questions.explanation: ARIA Classification (replaces old seasonal/perennial): BY DURATION: INTERMITTENT: Symptoms <4 days per week OR lasting <4 consecutive weeks. PERSISTENT: Symptoms ≥4 days per week AND lasting ≥4 consecutive weeks. BY SEVERITY: MILD: All of - normal sleep, normal daily activities, normal work/school, symptoms not troublesome. MODERATE-SEVERE: One or more of - sleep disturbance, impairment of daily activities/leisure/sport, impairment at work/school, troublesome symptoms. This classification guides treatment intensity. This patient: Symptoms year-round (persistent) + significant impact on work/sleep (moderate-severe) = PERSISTENT MODERATE-SEVERE AR. Also note: She has perennial allergens (dust mites, cat) + seasonal (tree, grass pollen), explaining year-round symptoms with spring worsening.

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You want to explain the mechanism to the patient.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of allergic rhinitis?

options.text: IgE-MEDIATED (Type I) hypersensitivity: SENSITIZATION: Allergen exposure → Th2 response → B-cells produce allergen-specific IgE → IgE binds mast cell FcεRI receptors; EARLY PHASE (minutes): Re-exposure → allergen cross-links IgE → mast cell degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → immediate symptoms (sneezing, itch, rhinorrhea); LATE PHASE (4-8 hours): Inflammatory cell recruitment (eosinophils, basophils, T-cells) → nasal congestion, ongoing symptoms
Type IV delayed hypersensitivity only
Non-immunologic mechanism
Complement-mediated reaction

questions.explanation: Allergic rhinitis pathophysiology: TYPE I (IgE-MEDIATED) HYPERSENSITIVITY: SENSITIZATION PHASE: Initial allergen exposure. APCs present allergen to Th2 cells. IL-4, IL-13 drive B-cell class switching to IgE. Allergen-specific IgE binds to FcεRI on mast cells (sensitization complete). EARLY PHASE RESPONSE (within minutes): Re-exposure to allergen. Allergen cross-links IgE on mast cells. Mast cell degranulation releases preformed mediators: Histamine → itching, sneezing, rhinorrhea, vasodilation. Tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes. LATE PHASE RESPONSE (4-8 hours later): Recruitment of inflammatory cells: Eosinophils, basophils, Th2 cells. Cytokine release (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13). Nasal congestion predominates. Priming effect - increased sensitivity. ALLERGIC MARCH: Eczema → AR → Asthma (this patient has all three!).

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has positive skin tests to multiple allergens.
questions.question: How is allergic rhinitis diagnosed?

options.text: Nasal biopsy is gold standard

CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS confirmed by allergy testing: HISTORY: Sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, pruritus (nose, eyes, palate); Symptom triggers and timing; Impact on QoL, sleep, work; EXAM: Pale/bluish boggy turbinates, clear discharge, allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines, nasal crease, conjunctival injection; ALLERGY TESTING: Skin prick testing (preferred) - confirms IgE sensitization; Serum specific IgE if skin testing not possible; MUST correlate test results with clinical history
CT sinus required for diagnosis
Diagnosis is purely clinical, no testing needed

questions.explanation: Diagnosing allergic rhinitis: CLINICAL HISTORY: Cardinal symptoms: Sneezing (paroxysmal), Rhinorrhea (watery, clear), Nasal congestion/obstruction, Nasal/ocular/palatal pruritus. Associated symptoms: Ocular (itching, tearing, redness), Post-nasal drip, Fatigue. Timing, triggers, seasonality, impact on QoL. PHYSICAL EXAM FINDINGS: Nasal: Pale, boggy, bluish turbinates; clear watery discharge; septal deviation. External: Allergic salute (rubbing nose upward), Transverse nasal crease, Allergic shiners (infraorbital darkening), Dennie-Morgan lines (infraorbital folds). Eyes: Conjunctival injection, chemosis, lid edema. Posterior pharynx: Cobblestoning (lymphoid hyperplasia). ALLERGY TESTING: SKIN PRICK TESTING: Gold standard, immediate results, higher sensitivity. SERUM SPECIFIC IgE: If skin testing contraindicated (severe eczema, antihistamine use, dermatographism). IMPORTANT: Positive test = sensitization. Must correlate with clinical symptoms for relevance!

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has persistent moderate-severe AR on oral antihistamine alone.
questions.question: What is the stepwise pharmacotherapy for allergic rhinitis?

options.text: ARIA 2024-2025 Stepwise Approach: MILD INTERMITTENT: Second-generation oral antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) OR intranasal antihistamine PRN; MODERATE-SEVERE or PERSISTENT: INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROID (INCS) first-line - fluticasone, mometasone, budesonide; INADEQUATE RESPONSE to INCS: Add intranasal antihistamine → INCS + INAH combination (MPAzeFlu); STILL UNCONTROLLED: Consider allergen immunotherapy (SCIT or SLIT)
Oral antihistamines are always first-line
Systemic corticosteroids for all moderate-severe AR
Oral decongestants are preferred long-term

questions.explanation: ARIA 2024-2025 Treatment Algorithm: STEP 1 - MILD INTERMITTENT: Second-generation oral antihistamine (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine, desloratadine) PRN. OR Intranasal antihistamine (azelastine, olopatadine) PRN. Avoid first-generation antihistamines (sedation, anticholinergic effects). STEP 2 - MODERATE-SEVERE or PERSISTENT: INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROID (INCS) - FIRST-LINE. Most effective class for AR. Options: fluticasone propionate/furoate, mometasone, budesonide, triamcinolone. Regular daily use, not PRN. STEP 3 - INADEQUATE RESPONSE TO INCS: Add intranasal antihistamine to INCS. OR Fixed combination: MPAzeFlu (azelastine + fluticasone propionate). Evidence shows INCS + INAH combination superior to either alone. STEP 4 - REFRACTORY: Consider allergen immunotherapy (SCIT or SLIT). Oral corticosteroid short course for severe exacerbations only. This patient: Persistent moderate-severe → should be on INCS, not just oral antihistamine.

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You want to explain why intranasal corticosteroids are preferred.
questions.question: How do intranasal corticosteroids compare to oral antihistamines?

options.text: INCS SUPERIOR to oral antihistamines for: Nasal congestion (oral AH minimally effective), Overall nasal symptoms (TNSS), Ocular symptoms, Quality of life; INCS address INFLAMMATION (underlying pathology); Oral antihistamines only block histamine (one mediator); Meta-analyses show INCS more effective for moderate-severe AR; INCS safe for long-term use - minimal systemic absorption; Onset: INCS may take days for full effect; oral AH faster initial relief
No difference between the classes
INCS should be avoided due to systemic effects
Oral antihistamines are more effective overall

questions.explanation: INCS vs Oral Antihistamines: EFFICACY COMPARISON (meta-analyses): INCS superior for: Total Nasal Symptom Score (TNSS), Nasal congestion specifically (oral AH have minimal effect on congestion!), Total Ocular Symptom Score, Rhinoconjunctivitis Quality of Life. Mean difference clinically meaningful. MECHANISM DIFFERENCE: INCS: Address underlying inflammation, Reduce multiple mediators (histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins, cytokines), Decrease inflammatory cell infiltration, Effects on both early and late phase. Oral Antihistamines: Block histamine H1 receptors only (one of many mediators), Better for sneezing, itching, rhinorrhea, Poor for nasal congestion. SAFETY: INCS: Minimal systemic absorption, Safe for long-term daily use, Local side effects (epistaxis, dryness) usually mild. ONSET: Oral AH: Faster initial symptom relief. INCS: May take several days for maximal effect (but more comprehensive control).

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient's symptoms remain inadequately controlled despite INCS.
questions.question: When is allergen immunotherapy indicated for allergic rhinitis?

options.text: INDICATIONS: Moderate-severe AR inadequately controlled with pharmacotherapy; IgE-mediated sensitization confirmed to relevant allergen(s); Patient motivated for 3-5 year treatment course; OPTIONS: SCIT (subcutaneous): Buildup then monthly maintenance; SLIT (sublingual): Daily tablets or drops at home; Both disease-modifying - may prevent asthma development, provide lasting benefit after discontinuation; CONTRAINDICATIONS: Severe/uncontrolled asthma, Immunodeficiency, Pregnancy (don't initiate)
First-line for all allergic rhinitis
Contraindicated if patient has asthma
Only for patients who failed all medications

questions.explanation: Allergen Immunotherapy (AIT): INDICATIONS: Moderate-to-severe AR, Inadequate control with optimized pharmacotherapy, Confirmed IgE sensitization to clinically relevant allergen(s), Patient preference to reduce medication dependence, Prevention/treatment of allergic asthma. TYPES: SCIT (Subcutaneous): Traditional injections. Build-up phase (weekly) → Maintenance (monthly). 3-5 years of treatment. Risk of systemic reactions → given in medical setting. SLIT (Sublingual): Tablets or drops. Daily home administration. approved tablets: grass, ragweed, dust mite. Safer profile, rare systemic reactions. BENEFITS (Disease-Modifying): Reduces symptoms and medication use. Sustained benefit after stopping (unlike medications). May prevent progression to asthma in children with AR. May prevent new sensitizations. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Severe or uncontrolled asthma (FEV1 <70%), Uncontrolled immunodeficiency, Pregnancy (don't initiate - can continue if started). This patient: Good candidate - persistent moderate-severe AR, multiple relevant sensitizations, has mild asthma.

questions.stem: This patient also has asthma and history of eczema.
questions.question: What are the key comorbidities of allergic rhinitis?

options.text: Treating AR doesn't affect asthma

UNITED AIRWAY DISEASE: AR and asthma strongly linked - 80% of asthmatics have AR, 40% of AR patients have asthma; Treating AR improves asthma control; ALLERGIC MARCH: Atopic dermatitis → AR → Asthma (progression in childhood); OTHER COMORBIDITIES: Allergic conjunctivitis (most common), Chronic rhinosinusitis, Otitis media with effusion, Sleep disturbance, Cognitive impairment/decreased productivity; Always screen AR patients for asthma symptoms
AR has no relationship to asthma
Eczema is unrelated to AR

questions.explanation: AR Comorbidities: UNITED AIRWAY DISEASE: AR and asthma are manifestations of same systemic allergic inflammation. ~80% of asthmatics have AR. ~40% of AR patients have or will develop asthma. Treating AR improves asthma outcomes! ALLERGIC MARCH (Atopic March): Progressive development of allergic diseases in childhood: Atopic dermatitis (infancy) → Allergic rhinitis (childhood) → Asthma (later childhood/adolescence). Not all patients follow this pattern. This patient exemplifies the march: childhood eczema → current AR + asthma. ALLERGIC CONJUNCTIVITIS: Most common comorbidity. Present in majority of AR patients. Eye symptoms: itching, tearing, redness. Treat with antihistamine eye drops, INCS also helps. OTHER COMORBIDITIES: Chronic rhinosinusitis, Otitis media with effusion (especially children), Sleep disturbance (nasal congestion), Daytime fatigue/cognitive impairment, Reduced work/school productivity. CLINICAL IMPLICATION: Always ask AR patients about asthma symptoms and vice versa!

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has persistent moderate-severe AR with asthma comorbidity.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: No treatment change needed

Complete approach: (1) Classification: Persistent moderate-severe AR (year-round, significant QoL impact); (2) Confirmed diagnosis: Classic symptoms + exam + positive SPT to dust mites, cat, tree/grass pollen; (3) PHARMACOTHERAPY: Step up from oral AH alone → INTRANASAL CORTICOSTEROID daily (first-line for mod-severe); Add intranasal antihistamine if needed (INCS+INAH combination); Continue oral AH PRN for breakthrough; (4) Environmental control: Dust mite covers, HEPA filters, cat avoidance counseling; (5) Consider IMMUNOTHERAPY: Multiple relevant sensitizations, inadequate pharmacotherapy control - SLIT for dust mites + SCIT for pollens; (6) Monitor and optimize asthma treatment (united airway)
Continue oral antihistamine alone
Systemic corticosteroids for control

questions.explanation: Complete management for this patient: DIAGNOSIS: Persistent moderate-severe AR. Multiple relevant sensitizations: perennial (dust mites, cat) + seasonal (tree, grass). Comorbid mild asthma. STEP 1 - OPTIMIZE PHARMACOTHERAPY: Intranasal corticosteroid DAILY - first-line for mod-severe AR. Options: fluticasone furoate, mometasone, etc. Proper technique education. If inadequate response → add intranasal antihistamine (INCS + INAH combination). Can continue oral antihistamine for breakthrough/ocular symptoms. Consider antihistamine eye drops for conjunctivitis. STEP 2 - ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROL: Dust mite: Allergen-impermeable mattress/pillow covers, wash bedding in hot water weekly, reduce humidity. Cat: Discuss cat avoidance (difficult) or keeping cat out of bedroom, HEPA filters. Pollen: Close windows during high pollen days, shower after outdoor activities. STEP 3 - ALLERGEN IMMUNOTHERAPY: Candidate: Multiple relevant allergens, moderate-severe symptoms, inadequate pharmacotherapy control. Options: SLIT tablets (dust mite, grass) + SCIT for other allergens. Disease-modifying, may prevent asthma progression. STEP 4 - UNITED AIRWAY: Optimize asthma treatment. Good AR control will improve asthma control.

sources.text: StatPearls: Allergic Rhinitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected allergic rhinitis?

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in allergic rhinitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in allergic rhinitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in allergic rhinitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

A.misconception: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
A.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of allergic rhinitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

A.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
A.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
A.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.

D.guidance: Consider why Consider the differential diagnosis systematically is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Consider the differential diagnosis systematically because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: When managing allergic rhinitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
C.guidance: Consider why Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization.
correct.deeperWhy: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.
